question_text: In a circuit, a lamp and a motor are connected in parallel across a 6V power supply. The lamp has a resistance of 12Ω and draws a current of 0.5A. The motor draws a current of 1.2A. Which statement best explains why the motor draws more current than the lamp despite being connected to the same voltage?

options.label: A
options.text: The motor is a larger device so it needs more current to operate.

options.label: B
options.text: The motor has a lower resistance than the lamp, so more current flows through it according to Ohm's law (I = V/R).

options.label: C
options.text: The motor is connected closer to the power supply so it receives more voltage.

options.label: D
options.text: The lamp consumes some of the voltage, leaving less for the motor.

explanation: In parallel circuits, all components experience the same voltage. Since the motor draws more current at the same voltage, it must have lower resistance. Using Ohm's law (I = V/R), if V is constant, a smaller R means a larger I.
